mainmatter.p: Let s \colon B \to A and r \colon A \to B be a pair of maps. A homotopy h \colon r \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibits s as a section of r and r as a retraction of s.

Let f , g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H \colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=gx. We shall write 1_{ f } \colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{ f } x: \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
Let s \colon B \to A and r \colon A \to B be a pair of maps. A homotopy h \colon r \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibits s as a section of r and r as a retraction of s.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be maps equipped with sections s_f \colon B \to A and s_g \colon C \to B. The composite s_f \circ s_g \colon C \to A is a section of g \circ f \colon A \to C.
The witness of sectionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two sections.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be maps equipped with retractions r_f \colon B \to A and r_g \colon C \to B. The composite r_f \circ r_g \colon C \to A is a retraction of g \circ f \colon A \to C.
The witness of retractionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two retractions.
When s \colon A \to B is a section of r \colon B \to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f \colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be biinvertible maps. Then the composite g \circ f \colon A \to C is biinvertible, with sections and retractions given by composites of the component sections and retractions.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be quasi-invertible maps. Then the composite g \circ f \colon A \to C is quasi-invertible, with sectionhood and retractionhood witnesses given by those of the composites of the component sections and retractions.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{ B } and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon, we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.
Any quasi-invertible map f \colon A \to B is biinvertible.
The quasi-inverse to f is simultaneously a section and a retraction.

An inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f \circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse.
The fiber of a function f \colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a \colon A equipped with identifications p \colon fa=b.
Any quasi-invertible map f \colon A \to B is biinvertible.
The quasi-inverse to f is simultaneously a section and a retraction.
Let f \colon A \to B be a section of some r \colon B \to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f \colon A \to B is a retract of the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B }, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. Because propositions are closed under retracts, it suffices to show that the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B } is a proposition, which follows because f is contractible.
Let f \colon A \to B be a retraction of some s \colon B \to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of - \circ f at 1_{ A }.

Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( f \circ - \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( C \to A \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( C \to B \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a section.

Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a retraction r \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a section s \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be maps equipped with sections s_f \colon B \to A and s_g \colon C \to B. The composite s_f \circ s_g \colon C \to A is a section of g \circ f \colon A \to C.
The witness of sectionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two sections.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be maps equipped with retractions r_f \colon B \to A and r_g \colon C \to B. The composite r_f \circ r_g \colon C \to A is a retraction of g \circ f \colon A \to C.
The witness of retractionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two retractions.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {ap}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.
A biinverse to a function f \colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{ B } and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon, we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.
